title: etcd High Latency / etcd Cluster Unhealthy
symptoms: Alert: etcdHighCommitDurations or etcdHighFsyncDurations
kubectl commands slow or timing out (>5s for simple operations)
API server logs show 'etcdserver: request timed out'
Alert: etcdInsufficientMembers or etcdNoLeader
causes: Disk I/O latency on etcd nodes (etcd is very disk-sensitive)
etcd leader election loop (unstable network between control plane nodes)
etcd DB size too large (>2GB triggers performance warnings)
Insufficient memory causing etcd compaction lag
Clock drift between control plane nodes
investigation: # On control plane node (SSH): etcdctl --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379 --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key endpoint health
# Check etcd DB size: etcdctl ... endpoint status --write-out=table
remediation: For disk I/O: move etcd data to faster disk (NVMe), use dedicated disk not shared with OS
For large DB: compact and defrag: etcdctl compact <rev> && etcdctl defrag
For clock drift: fix NTP on control plane nodes, restart chronyd
For network partition: check physical network between control plane nodes
For memory: ensure etcd nodes have >8GB RAM, set --quota-backend-bytes=8589934592
For leader election storm: identify the problematic node, drain and restart it
escalation: etcd quorum loss makes the entire Kubernetes cluster read-only. This is a P1 cluster outage. Escalate immediately with control plane access.

title: Kubernetes API Server Overloaded / Slow
symptoms: kubectl commands taking >10 seconds
Alert: KubeAPIErrorBudgetBurn or KubeAPILatencyHigh
Services failing to get updates from API server (watch errors)
Operators and controllers stuck or falling behind
causes: High number of LIST/WATCH requests overwhelming API server
etcd slow (see RB-K8S-001)
API server pod resource-limited (OOMKill or CPU throttle)
Thundering herd after cluster-wide pod restart
Many operators doing unfiltered LIST operations
investigation: # API server metrics: curl https://localhost:6443/metrics | grep apiserver_request_duration
remediation: Restart API server (non-destructive on multi-control-plane): kubectl -n kube-system delete pod kube-apiserver-<node>
Increase API server --max-requests-inflight and --max-mutating-requests-inflight
Identify noisy clients: grep API server logs for high-frequency requestors
Rate-limit specific operators by adding resource quotas or flow control (APF)
If etcd is the bottleneck: address etcd issues (see RB-K8S-001)
escalation: API server degradation impacts all Kubernetes operations. Escalate if kubectl commands take >30s or fail consistently.

title: Node NotReady / Kubelet Failure
symptoms: kubectl get nodes shows node in NotReady state
Alert: KubeNodeNotReady firing
Pods on that node in Unknown state
New pods not scheduled to the node
causes: Kubelet crashed or hung (disk full, OOMKill, bug)
Node lost network connectivity to API server
Container runtime (containerd) crashed
Node overloaded (CPU/memory pressure)
Disk pressure (PID pressure or inotify limit)
remediation: Restart kubelet: SSH to node, 'systemctl restart kubelet'
Restart containerd: SSH to node, 'systemctl restart containerd'
If disk full: clean up /var/log, old container images: 'crictl rmi --prune'
If OOMKill: check memory-intensive pods, add limits, or add more RAM
If node unresponsive: reboot the node (coordinated with draining first if possible)
Drain node before reboot: kubectl drain <node> --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data
escalation: Multiple nodes NotReady simultaneously is a cluster-wide outage. Physical access may be required for unresponsive nodes.

title: Kubernetes Certificate Expiry / TLS Errors
symptoms: kubectl commands fail with 'certificate has expired or is not yet valid'
Alert: KubernetesApiServerCertificateExpiresInLessThan14Days
Nodes go NotReady due to kubelet cert expiry
API server logs show TLS handshake errors
causes: Cluster certificates expired (kubeadm default: 1 year)
Auto-rotation disabled or failed
System time skew causing cert validity issues
Manual cert rotation done incorrectly
investigation: # Check cert expiry (on control plane node SSH):
remediation: Rotate all kubeadm certs: SSH to control plane, 'kubeadm certs renew all'
Restart control plane pods after renewal: they must reload certs
On Flatcar: kubeadm runs in container — exec into the kubeadm container or host
Update kubeconfig after renewal: 'cp /etc/kubernetes/admin.conf ~/.kube/config'
For kubelet cert rotation: ensure --rotate-certificates=true in kubelet config
Enable auto-renewal via CronJob or kubeadm upgrade (upgrades also renew certs)
escalation: Cert expiry causes full cluster authentication failure. Emergency P1 requiring immediate control plane access.

title: Pod OOMKilled / Memory Limit Exceeded
symptoms: Pod in CrashLoopBackOff with 'OOMKilled' in Last State Reason
kubectl describe pod shows: 'Last State: Terminated Reason: OOMKilled'
Alert: KubePodOOMKilled
Application restarts frequently without other error
causes: Memory limit set too low for the workload
Memory leak in the application
Spike in traffic causing temporary memory surge
JVM/Go runtime not respecting cgroup memory limits
investigation: kubectl describe pod <name> -n <namespace>  # check Limits and Last State
remediation: Increase memory limit: kubectl -n <ns> edit deployment <name>, set resources.limits.memory
For JVM: add -XX:MaxRAMPercentage=75 JVM flag, set JAVA_TOOL_OPTIONS
For Go: set GOMEMLIMIT env var to 90% of limit
If memory leak: investigate heap dumps, update application version
Set HorizontalPodAutoscaler to scale before OOM: add memory target
escalation: Persistent OOMKill without limit increase causes service degradation. Escalate to application team for memory optimization.

title: Flatcar Container Linux Node Update / Reboot Loop
symptoms: Node rebooting unexpectedly during working hours
Multiple nodes rebooting simultaneously causing disruption
Pods evicted and not rescheduled properly
kubelet showing 'node has rebooted' in logs
causes: Container Linux Update Engine (locksmith) triggering automatic reboot after OS update
All nodes in same reboot window (no max-reboot-count configured)
Manual OS update applied without draining first
Failed OS update leaving node in inconsistent state
investigation: kubectl get nodes -o wide  # check KERNEL-VERSION for differences
remediation: Pause locksmith to prevent more reboots: SSH to node, 'systemctl stop locksmithd'
Set max simultaneous reboots: configure locksmith with --max-count=1
Use pod disruption budgets to protect workloads during reboots
Manual drain before reboot: kubectl drain <node> --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data
Reboot node: kubectl cordon <node>, SSH and reboot, then kubectl uncordon <node>
For stuck update: SSH to node, 'update_engine_client -update' or 'systemctl restart update-engine'
escalation: Uncoordinated node reboots can cause widespread pod disruption. Coordinate maintenance windows for OS updates on production clusters.

title: PersistentVolumeClaim Stuck in Pending
symptoms: kubectl get pvc shows PVC in Pending state
Pod stuck in ContainerCreating waiting for volume
kubectl describe pvc shows 'waiting for a volume to be created'
No matching PersistentVolume available
causes: StorageClass not found or misspelled
Storage provisioner (Rook-Ceph CSI) not running
Insufficient storage in Ceph pool (see RB-CEPH-004)
StorageClass is WaitForFirstConsumer but no pod scheduled yet
ResourceQuota limiting PVC creation
remediation: Verify StorageClass name in PVC spec matches exactly (case-sensitive)
Check default StorageClass: kubectl get sc | grep default
If provisioner not running: fix Rook-Ceph CSI (see RB-CEPH-005)
If ResourceQuota: kubectl describe resourcequota -n <namespace>
For WaitForFirstConsumer: create the Pod — PVC will provision when pod is scheduled
escalation: PVC stuck in Pending blocks pod startup. If storage provisioner cannot be fixed, escalate to storage team.

title: DNS Resolution Failures Inside Cluster / CoreDNS Issues
symptoms: Pods cannot resolve service names (e.g., 'could not resolve host: my-svc.default.svc.cluster.local')
Alert: CoreDNSDown or KubeDNSDown
kubectl exec into pod shows nslookup failing
Intermittent DNS failures under load
causes: CoreDNS pods not running
CoreDNS Corefile misconfiguration
ndots setting too high causing slow DNS (trying multiple search domains)
CoreDNS memory limit too low under high query load
Upstream DNS (/etc/resolv.conf on nodes) unreachable
investigation: # Test from a pod: kubectl run dnstest --image=busybox --rm -it -- nslookup kubernetes
remediation: Restart CoreDNS: kubectl -n kube-system rollout restart deploy/coredns
Scale up CoreDNS for load: kubectl -n kube-system scale deploy/coredns --replicas=3
Increase memory: kubectl -n kube-system edit deploy/coredns, set resources.limits.memory
Fix upstream DNS: check /etc/resolv.conf on nodes via SSH
Enable CoreDNS caching: add 'cache 300' to Corefile forward block
escalation: DNS failure breaks service discovery cluster-wide. If CoreDNS cannot be restored, pods cannot find any services. P1 incident.
